claims.claim: Excited about stablecoins & payments — clear product-market fit, cheapest way to move a dollar, payments switching off card rails
claims.label: confirmed

claims.claim: Excited about zero-knowledge / SNARKs / verifiable compute (Jolt, proving overhead dropping into practice)
claims.label: confirmed

claims.claim: Excited about AI + crypto autonomous agents — wallets, agents paying each other/for compute, needing crypto rails plus identity/auth between agents
claims.label: confirmed

claims.claim: Excited about proof of personhood / private decentralized identity (Sybil-resistant, privacy-preserving, World-style)
claims.label: confirmed

claims.claim: Excited about fully on-chain games — composability/modding when no one controls the server, highest throughput on-chain
claims.label: absent

claims.claim: Excited about L2 scaling & blockchain infrastructure — cheaper blockspace, light clients (Helios), scaling must keep accelerating
claims.label: confirmed

claims.claim: Concerned about memecoins / pure speculation as a purposeless casino that sets the industry back and isn't technically interesting
claims.label: confirmed

claims.claim: Excited about ETH as a network token with a real economic loop (issuance security budget + EIP-1559 burn capturing value), builder leader; not a price call
claims.label: confirmed

claims.claim: Risk-on: conviction in the technology, still early, leaning in and building aggressively amid regulatory momentum
claims.label: confirmed

missed_changes: Built and heavily promoted a new 7-category token taxonomy (network / company-backed / arcade / memecoin / security / collectible / asset-backed), with strong new emphasis on 'company-backed tokens' as a distinct, securities-like risk category — a major framework the prediction didn't anticipate
Newly championed 'arcade tokens' (games/loyalty) as the 'most underappreciated token type in crypto,' and framed app-token vs. stablecoin choice as a key design question
Did a deep dive on airdrop design as a distribution/incentive mechanism (verify-the-supply, Sybil resistance, continuous airdrops)
Sharpened ETH/network-token economic theory into 'decentralized network marketplaces' (equity-like ownership), pushing back on ETH-as-money/oil/commodity framings, DCF debates, and 'Bitcoin envy'
Introduced a conceptual distinction within decentralization between 'diffusion/distributed' and 'control' (credible neutrality without diffusion)
Advocated moving crypto past the two-company 'foundation' model toward DUNA legal structures
Took up open-platform stances beyond crypto: criticizing PC app stores and Apple 'liquid glass' as closing the open web, plus interest in AI coding-agent tooling/swarms
notes: Window spans 2025-01 to 2025-06. Every prediction claim he addressed was reaffirmed — no contradictions. Stablecoins explicitly confirmed ('increasingly obvious product-market fit for stablecoins'); ZK/Jolt, proof-of-personhood/World, and risk-on ('never been a better time to start a project in crypto,' '$500b GO button') are dominant themes. AI+crypto confirmed mainly via 'identity for agents is one of the most obvious future challenges.' L2/infra confirmed via Helios update and 'we'll never have enough blockspace.' ETH network-token economic-loop thesis (EIP-1559 burn) confirmed. Only 'fully on-chain games' is absent — game references in the window pivot to token classification (arcade/app tokens) and a brief EVE Frontier shout-out rather than the throughput/composability/no-one-controls-the-server thesis. Much of his actual output went into a new token-taxonomy framework not foreseen by the prediction.
